title: CS102 Multiple-Choice Quiz
description: Multiple-choice review questions generated from one semester of CS102 slide files.

questions.text: Why do the slides emphasize understanding data structures even when AI can generate code?

choices.text: Because AI tools cannot compile Java programs
Because debugging, adapting, testing, and choosing data structures require understanding
Because data structures are only useful in interviews
Because generated code is always slower than handwritten code

questions.text: Which pairing best describes the relationship between a data structure and an algorithm?

choices.text: A data structure stores or organizes data; an algorithm is a procedure for solving a problem
A data structure is Java-specific; an algorithm is language-independent
A data structure is always faster than an algorithm
A data structure is a loop; an algorithm is a class

questions.text: In the string-building demo, why is StringBuilder much faster than repeatedly concatenating String objects?

choices.text: StringBuilder automatically sorts characters
StringBuilder avoids repeatedly creating and copying immutable String objects
StringBuilder stores characters in the call stack only
StringBuilder uses recursion instead of iteration

questions.text: What does the Java compiler produce from a .java source file?

choices.text: Machine-specific executable code
Java bytecode in a .class file
A heap snapshot
A JVM installation

questions.text: What is stored in a primitive variable's memory location?

choices.text: The actual value of the variable
A pointer to the source file
A reference to an object on the heap
The name of the variable as text

questions.text: Where are Java arrays stored?

choices.text: Always on the call stack
Always in the source file
On the heap, in consecutive memory locations
In bytecode, not memory

questions.text: What is the base case in a recursive algorithm?

choices.text: The step that makes the problem larger
The trivial case that can be solved directly
The final line of the source file
The stack frame of the caller

questions.text: In the recursive factorial function shown in the slides, what is the recursive call for factorial(n) when n > 0?

choices.text: factorial(n + 1)
factorial(0)
factorial(n - 1)
factorial(n * n)

questions.text: Which statement about recursion and iteration matches the slides?

choices.text: Any problem solved recursively can also be solved iteratively, and vice versa
Recursive algorithms never use memory
Recursive implementations are always faster than iterative ones
Iteration cannot be used for linked lists

questions.text: Why is binary search asymptotically faster than linear search on sorted data?

choices.text: It checks every element twice
It repeatedly halves the remaining search space
It stores data in a linked list
It avoids comparisons entirely

questions.text: What is the time complexity of accessing an element by index in an array?

choices.text: O(log N)
O(N)

questions.text: Why is binary search generally not efficient on a linked list?

choices.text: Linked lists cannot store sorted data
Linked lists cannot contain numbers
Accessing an arbitrary middle element takes linear time
Linked lists must be circular

questions.text: What is an abstract data type (ADT)?

choices.text: A precise specification of values and operations, independent of implementation
A Java class that must use arrays
A data type that cannot be implemented
A method for drawing memory diagrams

questions.text: In a singly linked list with a tail reference, what is the time complexity of adding to the back?

choices.text: O(log N)
O(N)

questions.text: Why does searching a linked list take O(N) time in the worst case?

choices.text: The list must be resized first
Each node may need to be visited one at a time
Every node stores two values
The list is always unsorted

questions.text: What extra reference does each node in a doubly linked list usually store compared with a singly linked list?

choices.text: A reference to the array capacity
A reference to the previous node
A reference to the JVM stack frame
A reference to the list's hash code

questions.text: Why is using an iterator preferable to repeated get(i) calls when traversing a linked list?

choices.text: Iterators sort the list while traversing
Iterators can visit each node once in O(N) time
Iterators convert the list into an array first
Iterators avoid storing references

questions.text: In a circular linked list, what makes the list circular?

choices.text: The first node stores the list size
The last node's next reference points back to the first node
Every node stores a duplicate value
The list cannot be empty

questions.text: What is the difference between an ArrayList's size and capacity?

choices.text: Size is the array length; capacity is the number of stored elements
Size is the number of stored elements; capacity is the length of the underlying array
They are always equal
Capacity counts only non-null values after index 0

questions.text: What is the amortized time complexity of appending to an ArrayList when the backing array grows by multiplication?

choices.text: O(log N)
O(N)

questions.text: Why is adding or removing at an arbitrary position in an ArrayList usually O(N)?

choices.text: Elements may need to be shifted to preserve order
Java must recompile the class
The list must be converted into a linked list
The hash code must be recomputed for every element

questions.text: Which access rule describes a stack?

choices.text: First in, first out
Last in, first out
Random access by index
Highest priority first

questions.text: Which access rule describes a queue?

choices.text: First in, first out
Last in, first out
Smallest key first
Left child before right child

questions.text: Why do circular-array queue implementations use modulo arithmetic?

choices.text: To sort queue elements
To wrap front and rear indexes back to the beginning of the array
To detect duplicate values
To convert a queue into a stack

questions.text: Why is a stack useful for validating expressions with multiple types of brackets?

choices.text: It remembers the most recent unmatched opening bracket
It converts every bracket into an integer
It sorts the brackets alphabetically
It guarantees the expression has no operators

questions.text: In postfix notation, where does the operator appear?

choices.text: Before its operands
Between its operands
After its operands
On a separate line from its operands

questions.text: What is the purpose of Dijkstra's Shunting Yard algorithm in these slides?

choices.text: To convert infix expressions to postfix notation
To balance an AVL tree
To resize an array queue
To find connected components in a graph

questions.text: How is the height of a tree defined in the slides?

choices.text: The number of leaves in the tree
The height of the root, calculated from leaves upward
The number of edges in the entire graph
The number of children of the root

questions.text: What order does an inorder traversal of a binary tree use?

choices.text: Node, left subtree, right subtree
Left subtree, right subtree, node
Left subtree, node, right subtree
Right subtree, node, left subtree, left subtree again

questions.text: What data structure does the iterative tree-size algorithm in the slides use to remember unexplored nodes?

choices.text: A stack
A hash table
A sorted array
A priority queue

questions.text: What property must hold at every node in a binary search tree?

choices.text: All values in the left subtree are smaller, and all values in the right subtree are larger
Every node has exactly two children
All leaves must be at the same depth
The tree must be stored in an array

questions.text: What is the time complexity of find in a BST in terms of tree height H?

choices.text: O(log H)
O(H)

questions.text: When removing a BST node with two children, what value can replace it while preserving the BST property?

choices.text: Any leaf in the tree
Its inorder predecessor or inorder successor
The root value, regardless of position
The first value inserted into the tree

questions.text: How does dequeue() differ in a priority queue compared with a regular FIFO queue?

choices.text: It removes the oldest element
It removes the element with highest priority
It removes the newest element
It removes a random element

questions.text: What two properties must a binary heap maintain?

choices.text: Shape property and heap order property
BST property and AVL balance factor
Hash property and load factor
Inorder property and recursion property

questions.text: Why are binary heaps commonly implemented using arrays?

choices.text: Complete-tree structure lets parent and child positions be computed by index
Arrays prevent the need for comparisons
Arrays make every operation O(1) in the worst case
Arrays automatically remove duplicate priorities

questions.text: Which sorting algorithms are described as quadratic sorts in the slides?

choices.text: Merge sort, quick sort, and heap sort
Bubble sort, selection sort, and insertion sort
Binary search, DFS, and BFS
Hashing, chaining, and probing

questions.text: Why does merge sort run in O(n log n) time?

choices.text: Each level does O(n) merging work and there are O(log n) levels
It never compares values
It only works on arrays of size 2
It uses a hash table for every comparison

questions.text: What can make quick sort deteriorate to O(n^2) time?

choices.text: Choosing pivots that split the array very unevenly
Using recursion at all
Sorting integers rather than strings
Using a base case

questions.text: Why do we want a BST to remain balanced?

choices.text: To guarantee traversals take O(1) time
To keep find, add, and remove operations at O(log N) in the worst case
To make every node have two children
To avoid using comparisons

questions.text: What is the AVL tree balance requirement?

choices.text: Every node must have balance factor exactly 0
The heights of the two subtrees of any node cannot differ by more than 1
The tree must be a complete binary tree
The root must always be the median of all inserted values

questions.text: What is the cost of one AVL rotation according to the slides?

choices.text: O(log N)
O(N)
O(N log N)

questions.text: In a dictionary or map ADT, what is true about keys?

choices.text: Keys must be stored in sorted order
Keys are unique, and each key maps to at most one value
Keys must be integers
Keys are removed automatically after lookup

questions.text: What is a hash collision?

choices.text: A key maps to an array index outside the table
Two different keys map to the same table index
A table has no empty cells
A value is larger than its key

questions.text: What does rehashing do when a hash table becomes too full?

choices.text: Sorts keys alphabetically
Recomputes all stored positions for a larger table
Deletes all collisions
Converts all values into strings

questions.text: What is a graph made of?

choices.text: Vertices and edges
Only arrays and indexes
Stack frames and heap objects
Keys and values only

questions.text: Which representation stores, for each node, a list of adjacent nodes?

choices.text: Edge list
Adjacency matrix
Adjacency list
Implicit heap

questions.text: What is the main behavioral difference between DFS and BFS?

choices.text: DFS follows paths deeply before backtracking; BFS visits nodes by increasing distance from the start
DFS only works on weighted graphs; BFS only works on trees
DFS requires an adjacency matrix; BFS requires an edge list
DFS is O(1); BFS is O(N^2) for all graphs
